course_title: Early Years Curriculum and Pedagogical Foundations

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate understanding of core early years curricula and pedagogy concepts
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of young children and their learning
Design quality environments to foster creativity and curiosity in young children
Document childrens learning through detailed visual and text descriptions
Plan learning experiences based on young childrens interests to enhance young childrens learning
Demonstrate an awareness of a range of strategies to engage parents and care givers as partners in the educative process
Critically analyse early years education literature and draw implications for early years learning environments and values for children's learning

Work effectively and collaboratively in teams to make informed team decisions, demonstrate interpersonal, leadership and communication skills, and critically evaluate personal and peer contributions to team processes.

assessments.assessment_title: Plan an early years learning activity
assessments.task_description:
You and a teaching partner have been asked to plan a short activity (approx. 7 minutes) for a small group of Prep students that focuses on one of the following concepts:

  • Early numeracy (such as a number concept)
  • Early literacy (such as familiarity with book characters)
  • Social and emotional development (such as exploring working together)
  • Oral language development (such as building vocabulary)
  • Play-based inquiry (such as investigating how objects move)

In this assessment, you will work collaboratively to design a developmentally appropriate learning experience for a small group (4-6 students) in a Prep (Foundation) classroom. The task is designed to help you apply principles of early years curriculum and pedagogy, with a particular focus on play-based learning, intentional teaching, and inclusive practice

Working in pairs, you will produce a written activity plan that includes the following components:

1. Learning Focus and Curriculum Links

  • Clearly identify the concept you have chosen, and explain why this concept is important in early years learning, drawing on relevant theory and research.
  • Identify and justify links to relevant curriculum frameworks (e.g., Australian Curriculum, EYLF, QKLG), and explain how your activity supports specific learning outcomes.

2. Activity Plan

  • Provide a detailed description of your planned activity, including
  • Learning intentions and success criteria
  • Materials and resources
  • Step-by-step teaching procedure
  • Teacher role (including intentional teaching strategies)
  • Opportunities for play-based learning

3. Pedagogical Justification

  • Explain how your activity reflects effective early years pedagogy (e.g., play-based learning, student engagement, differentiation, inclusion), refer to relevant theoretical perspectives (e.g., Vygotsky, Piaget, contemporary play-based learning approaches).
  • Describe how you will assess student learning during and/or after the activity (e.g., observation, questioning), and explain how assessment informs future teaching.


APSTs Assessed: 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 4.1, 6.3


A note about group work:

You will receive a single group mark for this task, as all group members should contribute equally to the design and delivery of the assessment task. However, exceptions can be made in the event of one or more group members engaging in ‘social loafing’ (also known as ‘free-riding’):

Free riding is a form of social loafing observed in groups when one or more members slack off and ‘ride’ on the extra efforts of their co-workers (Walker & Angelo, 1998).

If your small group is experiencing dysfunction, including ‘social loafing’ by one or more members, it is your responsibility to bring this promptly to the attention of your tutor/course coordinator, who will then support the group to resolve the problems in a respectful way. If no resolution can be found, the tutor/course coordinator can take steps, including removing the non-contributing student(s) from the group and requiring the student(s) to complete the task either individually or in another small group with other ‘social loafers’.


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please use the Turnitin Submission link on Blackboard to upload your task as a Word document. Ideally, 11pt font with 1.5 line spacing.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Teach an early years learning activity
assessments.task_description:
Building on your planned activity from Task 1, you and your teaching partner will now teach it to a small group of peers who will act as Prep students. This task focuses on your ability to translate planning into practice, using effective early years pedagogies such as play-based learning, intentional teaching, and responsive interaction. It also includes a structured peer interaction component, where you respond to questions and engage in professional discussion about your teaching decisions.

Part A: Teaching the Activity (5-7 minutes)

Working in pairs, you will deliver your planned activity. During your teaching, you are expected to:

  • Communicate the learning intention clearly and in an age-appropriate way
  • Use intentional teaching strategies (e.g., questioning, modelling, scaffolding, feedback)
  • Facilitate play-based learning opportunities
  • Demonstrate effective engagement and interaction strategies
  • Use appropriate oral language and communication for Prep learners
  • Respond flexibly to student contributions


Part B: Peer Interaction and Q&A (3-5 minutes)

Following your teaching, you will participate in a short question-and-answer discussion with peers and the tutor. You will be expected to:

  • Explain the purpose and learning focus of your activity
  • Justify your pedagogical choices (e.g., play-based approach, teaching strategies)
  • Describe how your activity aligns with curriculum outcomes
  • Suggest how the activity could be adapted for diverse learners
  • Respond clearly and professionally to questions and feedback


APSTs Assessed: 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 4.1, 6.3


A note about group work:

You will receive a single group mark for this task, as all group members should contribute equally to the design and delivery of the assessment task. However, exceptions can be made in the event of one or more group members engaging in ‘social loafing’ (also known as ‘free-riding’):

Free riding is a form of social loafing observed in groups when one or more members slack off and ‘ride’ on the extra efforts of their co-workers (Walker & Angelo, 1998).

If your small group is experiencing dysfunction, including ‘social loafing’ by one or more members, it is your responsibility to bring this promptly to the attention of your tutor/course coordinator, who will then support the group to resolve the problems in a respectful way. If no resolution can be found, the tutor/course coordinator can take steps, including removing the non-contributing student(s) from the group and requiring the student(s) to complete the task either individually or in another small group with other ‘social loafers’.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentation will occur during class time in Weeks 8 and 9.

assessments.assessment_title: Resource file – Early years documentation & planning
assessments.task_description:
To complete your resource file:

1. Choose one of the profiles provided and use the available data sets (e.g., community profile, transitions statements, and learning information) to identify a learning episode in an Early Years setting.

2. Document the learning episode using selected evidence from the provided data, presenting in a family-engaging manner as per Reggio Emilio principles. The commentary should:

  • celebrate the learning and articulate the value of the learning
  • demonstrate how data has informed your interpretation of the learning
  • make reference to concepts in course readings, relevant curriculum documents and further early childhood education literature
  • show how documentation can be used to communicate learning to families.
  • provide some commentary on extending the student’s learning for next steps in their development.

3. Plan a project from the learning needs, strengths, and interests identified through your data analysis. Follow the project approach structure of Phase 1, 2 and 3 discussed in Young investigators, including: noting questions and possible happenings and possible ways to conclude and share the project. The experience you plan will build on children’s interests, knowledge and skills and inspire authentic integrated learning. The plan must include enough detail so that another educator could implement the project using the plan. Use course content (pedagogy, policies, ethical considerations, curricula) to inform your decision-making of teaching strategies, intended learning outcomes and resources incorporated.

4. Ensure that you include: details on the environment (physical & social), teaching strategies, resources and address relevant (to observed context) curriculum statements or descriptions. Examples will be explored in lectures and tutorials are designed for your to practice identifying elements to include in a plan that considers pedagogy of place.

5. Include reference list in APA 7th style. 

APSTs Assessed: 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.7, 4.1


If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library
